items.why: Removing the first element of a list shifts every other element — O(n) per call; over many calls it's O(n²).
items.example:
```python
from collections import deque
q = deque(jobs)
while q:
    handle(q.popleft())     # O(1) per dequeue
```

items.mistake: Using a stack when order of insertion must be preserved (e.g. streaming text).
items.fix: Use a queue (FIFO) so consumers see items in arrival order.
items.why: A stack reverses order on the way out; a streamed completion would render backwards.
items.example:
```python
# Wrong: stack reverses order
for chunk in reversed_stream:
    stack.append(chunk)
while stack:
    render(stack.pop())     # reversed!
```

items.fix: Negate the priority on push; negate again on pop (or use `heapq.nlargest`).
items.why: `heapq` only implements a *min*-heap; for highest-first behavior you must flip the sign.
items.example:
```python
import heapq
pq = []
heapq.heappush(pq, (-score, item))   # max-heap by score
-(-s), item = heapq.heappop(pq)
```

items.mistake: Building an unbounded queue and letting it grow forever.
items.fix: Cap the queue (`deque(maxlen=N)` or `queue.Queue(maxsize=N)`) and apply backpressure.
items.why: An unbounded queue masks the fact that consumers can't keep up, eventually exhausting memory.
